user_mistake: first attempt: ENABL_EXTENDED_FLAGS (missing E) instead of ENABLE_EXTENDED_FLAGS. Retry attempt: lt__min_vk_to_lt_key (m vs w letter swap) instead of lt__win_vk_to_lt_key — same identifier-typo class, different surface
gap_action: the 'type these:' copy-block alone is insufficient when identifiers contain prefixes that are common English words (lt__win_*, lt__plat_*) or use visually similar letters (m/w/n, l/I/1, 0/O). Augment with: (a) for cross-file function names, an explicit 'this name must match byte-for-byte across N files: file1, file2' callout naming each file; (b) a grep command the user can run as a self-check after typing; (c) for prefixes that are English words, a one-line pronunciation cue ('lt-libterm-win as in Windows, not min')
topic: libterm Windows platform layer — close API gaps

user_mistake: added #include <consoleapi.h> to plat_init.c on a MinGW toolchain where that filename does not exist; should have stayed with <windows.h> umbrella
gap_action: before introducing any Win32 API in chat, state the include policy up front in one line: 'on Windows, only <windows.h> is needed; ignore MSDN per-function Header notes — those are MSVC SDK names that MinGW does not ship'. Repeat at every layer that touches new Win32 symbols, not just the first
topic: libterm Windows platform layer — close API gaps

user_mistake: Ctrl+C still terminates hello.c after Layer 5 changes — clearing ENABLE_PROCESSED_INPUT alone is insufficient
gap_action: when teaching any Windows console-input gating, lead with the two-layer model first: (1) console input queue (ENABLE_PROCESSED_INPUT, ReadConsoleInputW) and (2) process control-signal pipeline (SetConsoleCtrlHandler, default ExitProcess). Only after both layers are named should specific flags be introduced. The same pattern applies to mouse input, console-window resize, and process termination — all have queue-side and signal-side mechanisms
topic: libterm Windows platform layer — close API gaps

user_mistake: first attempt typed `in_mode |= ENABLE_QUICK_EDIT_MODE;` (set instead of clear); retry attempt typed `in_mode &= ENABLE_QUICK_EDIT_MODE;` (missing `~`, masks to only-this-bit) — set/clear operator confusion persists across two corrective rounds
gap_action: for any single-bit clear, present three artifacts together: (1) full statement in a code block, (2) bit-pattern diff table showing `&= ~FLAG` vs the wrong `&= FLAG` mask outcomes side by side, (3) an explicit 'read your line out loud as: AND-equals tilde FLAG_NAME' verbalization cue. The `~` is one character and visually dropable; multi-modal reinforcement makes it stick
topic: libterm Windows platform layer — close API gaps

user_mistake: in bench_present.c qpc_init, typed QueryPerformanceCounter instead of QueryPerformanceFrequency. The names differ by one word but the semantics are entirely different — one returns the current tick value, the other returns the ticks-per-second rate. Wrong call ran clean, gave nonsense bench numbers (~0.1 µs total for 1000 iterations) for three consecutive runs before the typo was caught
gap_action: for Win32 API pairs whose names differ by one trailing word but whose semantics are unrelated (QueryPerformanceCounter / QueryPerformanceFrequency, GetCurrentDirectory / SetCurrentDirectory, GetConsoleMode / SetConsoleMode), always state the role distinction in the layer's prose before showing code: 'we want the rate, not the current value — those are two different APIs, the one we want is the *Frequency* not the *Counter*.' Repeat the role distinction in a comment if practical. Don't trust pattern-match-from-context to pick the right one

user_mistake: when applying the sink-XOR change to bench_present.c, the bench_full_repaint loop lost its lt_present call entirely — the original `(void)lt_present();` was supposed to become `lt__bench_sink ^= lt_present();` but instead got dropped from that one of three loops. Other two loops were updated correctly. Resulted in scenario (c) measuring only paint_full memory writes with no rendering
gap_action: when prescribing a 'replace every X with Y across N occurrences' edit, provide the exact list of occurrence sites by line number or function name, not just the rule. The user's edit-attention drops on the third or later instance of a multi-site pattern. Show: 'three sites — bench_no_change line N1, bench_one_cell line N2, bench_full_repaint line N3' with a checklist framing rather than a single rule
